role: Producer (Johnny Cash, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Adele)
quote: The best compression is when you don't hear it. When you hear compression, something has gone wrong.
source: The Creative Act: A Way of Being

role: Producer (Johnny Cash, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Adele)
quote: The goal is not to make a record that sounds like other records. The goal is to make a record that sounds like itself.
source: The Creative Act: A Way of Being

role: Producer (Johnny Cash, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Adele)
quote: Listening is the most important skill a producer can have. More important than any technical knowledge.
source: The Creative Act: A Way of Being

role: Producer (Johnny Cash, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Adele)
quote: When something sounds exciting, it usually means the dynamics are working. Excitement lives in the contrast between loud and soft.
source: The Creative Act: A Way of Being

role: Mix Engineer (Adele, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Beyoncé)
quote: Parallel compression is about preserving the life of the transients while still controlling the overall level. You get the punch and the sustain at the same time.
source: Sound On Sound — In The Studio With Andrew Scheps, February 2014

role: Mix Engineer (Adele, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Beyoncé)
quote: I never EQ into a problem. I EQ out of one. If you need a lot of EQ, something went wrong before it got to the mix.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 100, 2014

role: Mix Engineer (Adele, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Beyoncé)
quote: The low end is where most mixes fall apart. If the kick and bass aren't working together, nothing else matters.
source: Sound On Sound — In The Studio With Andrew Scheps, February 2014

role: Mix Engineer (Bruce Springsteen, The Rolling Stones, Bryan Adams)
quote: Reverb is not decoration. It's the room the music lives in. Get the room wrong and the music feels homeless.
source: Sound On Sound — Classic Tracks: Bryan Adams Run To You, March 2010

role: Mix Engineer (Bruce Springsteen, The Rolling Stones, Bryan Adams)
quote: I use compression to make things feel right, not to make them louder. There's a difference and most people confuse the two.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 78, 2010

role: Mix Engineer (Bruce Springsteen, The Rolling Stones, Bryan Adams)
quote: Automation is the difference between a mix that works and a mix that moves. A static mix is a dead mix.
source: Sound On Sound — Bob Clearmountain Interview, January 2008

role: Producer (Michael Jackson, Frank Sinatra, Ray Charles)
quote: Leave some room for God to walk through the room. Don't fill every space.
source: Quincy Jones — Q: The Autobiography of Quincy Jones

role: Producer (Michael Jackson, Frank Sinatra, Ray Charles)
quote: The kick drum and the bass guitar must be married. They are one instrument in two parts.
source: Sound On Sound — The Making of Thriller, October 2001

role: Recording Engineer (Frank Sinatra, Paul McCartney, Diana Krall)
quote: I've always believed that less EQ is more. The microphone choice and placement should do most of the work before you ever touch an equalizer.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 56, 2006

role: Recording Engineer (Frank Sinatra, Paul McCartney, Diana Krall)
quote: Headroom is respect for the music. You leave space because you never know when something beautiful is going to happen.
source: Sound On Sound — Al Schmitt: A Life In Recording, June 2015

role: Producer (U2, David Bowie, Talking Heads)
quote: Treat noise as signal. Every accident in the studio is an invitation.
source: A Year With Swollen Appendices — Brian Eno's Diary

role: Producer (U2, David Bowie, Talking Heads)
quote: Reverb is a way of placing sound in imaginary space. You're not just adding effect — you're building a world.
source: Sound On Sound — Brian Eno Interview, December 2004

role: Producer (U2, David Bowie, Talking Heads)
quote: Oblique strategies exist because the most useful thing you can do when stuck is to change the frame, not push harder.
source: A Year With Swollen Appendices — Brian Eno's Diary

role: Recording Engineer (Nirvana, Pixies, PJ Harvey)
quote: Compression is the enemy of dynamics and dynamics are what make music feel alive. Use it sparingly or don't use it at all.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 9, 1998

role: Recording Engineer (Nirvana, Pixies, PJ Harvey)
quote: The room is an instrument. If you don't treat it that way you're throwing away half your recording.
source: Sound On Sound — Steve Albini: The Man Behind The Music, April 2012

role: Recording Engineer (Nirvana, Pixies, PJ Harvey)
quote: Gain staging is not optional. It is the foundation of every recording. Get it wrong and you spend the rest of the session chasing ghosts.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 9, 1998

role: Mix Engineer (Green Day, Muse, My Chemical Romance)
quote: Bus compression glues a mix the way cement holds bricks. Without it the elements are just sitting next to each other.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 63, 2007

role: Mix Engineer (Green Day, Muse, My Chemical Romance)
quote: I want every element to have its own space in the stereo field. Stereo imaging is about giving everything a home.
source: Sound On Sound — Mix Masters: Chris Lord-Alge, September 2009

role: Mix Engineer (Dave Matthews Band, Alanis Morissette, Smash Mouth)
quote: Saturation adds the harmonic richness that digital recording takes away. It's not distortion — it's life.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 71, 2009

role: Mix Engineer (John Mayer, Gwen Stefani, The Black Eyed Peas)
quote: EQ is sculpting. You're revealing what's already there, not adding something new. Cutting is almost always better than boosting.
source: Sound On Sound — Mix Masters: Jack Joseph Puig, March 2008

role: Mix Engineer (Tom Waits, Peter Gabriel, Arctic Monkeys)
quote: Distortion is character. Everything I've ever loved sonically has had some degree of harmonic distortion in it.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 44, 2004

role: Mix Engineer (Tom Waits, Peter Gabriel, Arctic Monkeys)
quote: Mid-side processing is one of the most powerful tools in mixing. It lets you treat the center and the width as completely separate entities.
source: Sound On Sound — Tchad Blake Interview, May 2011

role: Producer/Engineer (Nine Inch Nails, U2, Depeche Mode)
quote: Noise gates are blunt instruments. I'd rather ride the fader than gate something and lose the natural decay.
source: Sound On Sound — Flood: Engineering The Future, November 2013

role: Producer/Engineer (Nine Inch Nails, U2, Depeche Mode)
quote: Delay is a rhythmic instrument, not an effect. When it's timed to the track it becomes part of the groove.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 88, 2012

role: Producer (Kanye West, Travis Scott, Jay-Z)
quote: Sidechain compression isn't just a pumping effect — it creates space. When the kick hits, everything moves out of the way.
source: Rolling Stone — The Architects of Hip-Hop Sound, March 2019

role: Producer (Kanye West, Travis Scott, Jay-Z)
quote: 808s changed everything. That's a synthesized bass note, not a kick drum. When you understand that, you understand how to mix it.
source: Sound On Sound — Mike Dean: The Architect, January 2020

role: Producer (Radiohead, Thom Yorke, Beck)
quote: We use limiting as a creative tool, not a safety net. The ceiling you set changes the character of everything below it.
source: Sound On Sound — Radiohead: OK Computer Revisited, June 2017

role: Producer (Radiohead, Thom Yorke, Beck)
quote: Automation is the life of the mix. Without it you have a photograph. With it you have a film.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 55, 2006

role: Producer (Nirvana, Smashing Pumpkins, Foo Fighters)
quote: When Nevermind came out people said the drums sounded like cannons. That's just what happens when you compress a great drummer in a great room.
source: Sound On Sound — The Making of Nevermind, September 2011

role: Producer (Nirvana, Smashing Pumpkins, Foo Fighters)
quote: Layering guitars is about finding the frequency gaps. Every layer should live in a different part of the spectrum.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 17, 2000

role: Mix Engineer (Beyoncé, Christina Aguilera, Michael Jackson)
quote: Parallel compression is my secret weapon on vocals. The dry signal keeps the intimacy, the compressed signal keeps the power.
source: Pensado's Place — Episode 200: Dave Pensado on Compression

role: Mix Engineer (Beyoncé, Christina Aguilera, Michael Jackson)
quote: If your gain staging is wrong, every plugin after it is fighting the same battle. Sort it at the top and everything downstream gets easier.
source: Pensado's Place — Episode 185: Gain Staging Deep Dive

role: Mix Engineer (Beyoncé, Christina Aguilera, Michael Jackson)
quote: The best EQ moves are the ones you make and then forget about. Drastic EQ is a symptom of a problem, not a solution to one.
source: Pensado's Place — Episode 210: EQ Masterclass

role: Producer/Engineer (Tool, System of a Down, Red Hot Chili Peppers)
quote: I've recorded vocals through guitar amps, mic'd from the other room, in stairwells — because the room is part of the tone. Reverb is just a room you choose.
source: Recording Unhinged — Creative and Unconventional Music Recording Techniques

role: Producer/Engineer (Tool, System of a Down, Red Hot Chili Peppers)
quote: Distortion is emotional. Clean is clinical. Most of the music that moves people has some form of harmonic distortion in it.
source: Recording Unhinged — Creative and Unconventional Music Recording Techniques

role: Mix Engineer (Beyoncé, Jay-Z, John Legend)
quote: The stereo field is real estate. You have left, right, and center. Don't crowd them. Give every element its own address.
source: Sound On Sound — Mix Masters: Tony Maserati, July 2010

role: Mix Engineer (Beyoncé, Jay-Z, John Legend)
quote: A limiter on the master bus is not the same as mastering. It's a safety net. Real mastering is a separate creative act.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 80, 2010

role: Producer (Adele, Katy Perry, OneRepublic)
quote: Arrangement is compression in disguise. When you strip elements out, the ones that remain hit harder. Space is dynamic range.
source: MasterClass — Greg Wells Teaches Music Production

role: Producer (Adele, Katy Perry, OneRepublic)
quote: The chorus has to feel like a release. That means the verse has to feel like tension. Dynamic range is not just technical — it's emotional.
source: MasterClass — Greg Wells Teaches Music Production

role: Producer/Songwriter (Taylor Swift, Katy Perry, The Weeknd)
quote: A great song doesn't need a great mix. But a great mix can save a good song. The production is not separate from the writing.
source: Billboard — Max Martin: The Man Behind The Hits, January 2016

role: Producer (Missy Elliott, Justin Timberlake, Jay-Z)
quote: FM synthesis gave me sounds nobody had heard before. That metallic, clangy quality — that's frequency modulation doing things subtractive can't.
source: Sound On Sound — Timbaland: The Sound Architect, March 2007

role: Producer (Missy Elliott, Justin Timberlake, Jay-Z)
quote: The kick is the foundation. Everything else is decoration. If I can't feel it in my chest, the mix isn't done.
source: Rolling Stone — Timbaland: The Beat Architect, May 2006

role: Producer (Snoop Dogg, Eminem, Kendrick Lamar)
quote: Sub bass is felt before it's heard. If you can't feel it on small speakers, you don't have low end — you have the idea of low end.
source: Sound On Sound — The Science of West Coast Hip-Hop, August 2012

role: Producer (Beyoncé, Jay-Z, Snoop Dogg)
quote: Space is the instrument nobody talks about. What you leave out of a track is just as important as what you put in.
source: Rolling Stone — Pharrell Williams: The Sound of Everything, September 2013

role: Producer (Beyoncé, Jay-Z, Snoop Dogg)
quote: Sampling is archaeology. You're finding something buried in a record from 1972 and giving it new life in a different context.
source: Billboard — Pharrell Williams Interview, March 2014

role: Artist/Producer
quote: Distortion is not a guitar thing. Distortion is an everything thing. When in doubt, run it through something broken.
source: Sound On Sound — Nine Inch Nails: The Fragile Sessions, October 1999

role: Artist/Producer (Nine Inch Nails)
quote: Automation is not mixing — it's performing. Every move you make in the automation is a performance decision.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 39, 2003

role: Recording Engineer (The Beatles)
quote: We were always looking for ways to put the microphone where it had never been before. Close-miking a bass cabinet — that was considered radical in 1966.
source: Here, There and Everywhere: My Life Recording the Music of the Beatles

role: Recording Engineer (The Beatles)
quote: Limiting was our secret weapon at Abbey Road. We used to pin the limiters hard and the transients that came through had a character nothing digital can replicate.
source: Here, There and Everywhere: My Life Recording the Music of the Beatles

role: Recording Engineer (David Bowie, The Beatles, Elton John)
quote: A great recording starts before you press record. The arrangement, the performance, the room — those are your raw materials. Everything after is just managing what you captured.
source: Abbey Road to Ziggy Stardust — Ken Scott Memoir

role: Producer (The Cure, U2, Manic Street Preachers)
quote: Reverb is memory. When you add reverb you're adding the acoustic memory of a space — real or imagined.
source: Sound On Sound — Mike Hedges: Producing The Cure, April 2004

role: Mix Engineer (Nine Inch Nails, Smashing Pumpkins, My Bloody Valentine)
quote: Chorus and phaser are time-based illusions. You're making one source sound like many, or making something static sound like it's breathing.
source: Sound On Sound — Alan Moulder: The Art of Noise, February 2009

role: Mix Engineer (Nine Inch Nails, Smashing Pumpkins, My Bloody Valentine)
quote: Shoegaze is just guitar through every modulation effect at once. The wall of sound is a wall of choruses and flangers fighting each other beautifully.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 92, 2012

role: Producer/Engineer (The Shins, Morrissey, Beck)
quote: I use EQ to tell a story. The high end is the air and the detail. The low mid is the warmth and the weight. Every boost or cut is a narrative choice.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 58, 2007

role: Producer/Engineer (The Shins, Morrissey, Beck)
quote: Vocal production is psychology as much as it is engineering. The singer has to feel safe before you can get a great performance.
source: Sound On Sound — Joe Chiccarelli: Producing The Human Voice, August 2011

role: Mix Engineer (James Taylor, Foreigner, Carly Simon)
quote: Mastering is the last creative decision in the chain. A good mastering engineer hears things in a mix that the mixer stopped hearing three days ago.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 34, 2002

role: Mastering Engineer — author of Mastering Audio
quote: LUFS is not a creative standard, it's a delivery standard. The creative decisions about loudness should happen long before the master.
source: Mastering Audio: The Art and the Science — Third Edition

role: Mastering Engineer — author of Mastering Audio
quote: The loudness war destroyed dynamic range in music for twenty years. LUFS normalization gave it back. Now the question is whether producers will use that space.
source: Mastering Audio: The Art and the Science — Third Edition

role: Mastering Engineer — author of Mastering Audio
quote: Headroom is not wasted space. It's potential energy. The transients that live in that headroom are what make a mix feel powerful.
source: Mastering Audio: The Art and the Science — Third Edition

role: Mastering Engineer — founder of Production Advice
quote: Loudness normalisation means the loudness war is over. If you master loud, the platform turns you down. Dynamics are free again.
source: Production Advice — The Death of the Loudness War, 2015

role: Author — Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio
quote: If you can't hear the difference between your room and a flat response, you're mixing in the dark. Treatment is not optional for serious work.
source: Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio — Second Edition

role: Author — Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio
quote: Subtractive EQ should always come before additive EQ. Remove the problem frequencies first — what you want to add may already be there, just buried.
source: Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio — Second Edition

role: Author — The Art of Mixing
quote: Every element in a mix occupies a position in a three-dimensional space: left-right, front-back, and up-down. Mixing is placing sounds in that space.
source: The Art of Mixing: A Visual Guide to Recording, Engineering, and Production

role: Recording Engineer (Jimi Hendrix, Led Zeppelin, The Beatles)
quote: Jimi heard distortion as a musical element, not a side effect. He was using harmonic distortion as a compositional tool before anyone had a name for it.
source: Sound On Sound — Eddie Kramer: The Hendrix Sessions, November 2010

role: Recording Engineer (Jimi Hendrix, Led Zeppelin, The Beatles)
quote: John Bonham's room sound on When The Levee Breaks was three microphones and a great drummer in a stone stairwell. No plugin will ever fully recreate what that room did.
source: Sound On Sound — The Making of Led Zeppelin IV, December 2007

role: Mix Engineer (Mark Knopfler, Dire Straits, George Strait)
quote: The send-return architecture is one of the most elegant things about mixing. You can put a little of anything anywhere without committing to it.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 66, 2008

role: Mix Engineer (Mark Knopfler, Dire Straits, George Strait)
quote: Surround sound taught me how important the center channel is in stereo. Everything that matters lives in the middle.
source: Sound On Sound — Chuck Ainlay: Mixing In Surround, May 2006

role: Mix Engineer (Kanye West, Rihanna, John Legend)
quote: Parallel compression on drums gives you the attack of the uncompressed signal and the sustain of the squashed one. It's the best of both worlds.
source: Sound On Sound — Mix Masters: Manny Marroquin, October 2012

role: Mix Engineer (Kanye West, Rihanna, John Legend)
quote: I automate everything. Volume, EQ, reverb sends — every element of the mix breathes with the song because of automation.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 95, 2013

role: Producer (Amy Winehouse, Bruno Mars, Lady Gaga)
quote: Sampling is not laziness — it's curation. Finding the right eight bars from a 1968 soul record and building a new world around it is a creative act.
source: Rolling Stone — Mark Ronson: The Art of the Sample, February 2015

role: Producer (Major Lazer, Justin Bieber, Beyoncé)
quote: Bass is a global language. Every culture responds to low frequency. When I'm making music for the world, I start with the bass.
source: Rolling Stone — Diplo: The Producer Who Ate The World, April 2016

role: Producer/Artist (Rihanna, Dua Lipa, Sam Smith)
quote: Sidechaining the bass to the kick is not a stylistic choice in dance music — it's a structural requirement. The groove depends on it.
source: DJ Mag — Calvin Harris: The Science of the Drop, March 2018

role: Producer/Artist (Justin Bieber, Diplo, Ellie Goulding)
quote: Distortion and synthesis are the same conversation. You're shaping harmonics either way — one just starts with a waveform and the other starts with a clean signal.
source: Sound On Sound — Skrillex: The Architecture of Bass, January 2013

role: Producer/Artist
quote: Sidechain pumping is overused. But when it's right — when the whole mix breathes with the kick — there's nothing more satisfying in electronic music.
source: Attack Magazine — Deadmau5: On Production, September 2012

role: Producer/Artist
quote: LFOs are the heartbeat of a synthesizer. Everything that feels alive in a synth patch has an LFO somewhere doing the breathing.
source: Sound On Sound — Deadmau5: Synthesis Deep Dive, March 2014

role: Producer (Kendrick Lamar, Thundercat, Erykah Badu)
quote: Sampling taught me to listen differently. You hear a record and instead of hearing the song, you hear components — the snare, the room, the chord — separately.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 103, 2014

role: Producer (Gnarls Barkley, Beck, Black Keys)
quote: Lo-fi is not a technical limitation — it's an aesthetic choice. Saturation and tape compression add a warmth that says 'this was made by a human being.'
source: Rolling Stone — Danger Mouse: The Analog Mind in a Digital World, June 2011

role: Producer (Gnarls Barkley, Beck, Black Keys)
quote: The best arrangements feel inevitable. Every element is exactly where it needs to be and nothing is fighting for space.
source: Sound On Sound — Danger Mouse: The Art of Arrangement, February 2012

role: Producer/Artist (Brian Eno, King Creosote, Coldplay)
quote: Reverb tails are where the emotion lives. The note ends but the room keeps speaking. That's where the feeling comes from.
source: Sound On Sound — Jon Hopkins: Sound and Space, November 2018

role: Producer/Artist
quote: I'm always automating the filter. The frequency sweep is the melody. In electronic music, movement in the frequency domain is as musical as pitch.
source: Resident Advisor — Four Tet: Process and Product, October 2015

role: Producer/Artist
quote: FM synthesis is the most complex simple thing in electronic music. Two oscillators, one modulating the other — and you have infinite timbral possibilities.
source: Sound On Sound — Aphex Twin: The Drukqs Sessions, December 2001

role: Artist/Producer
quote: Vocal production is about capturing the truth of a performance, then enhancing it. If you're trying to fix the performance with processing, you've already lost.
source: Sound On Sound — Imogen Heap: Self-Production, July 2009

role: Artist/Producer
quote: Auto-Tune is an instrument. Treating it as a corrective tool misses the point entirely. When you pitch-shift a voice to exact pitch at zero speed, you create something that never existed in nature.
source: Rolling Stone — T-Pain: In Defense of Auto-Tune, August 2014

role: Producer (Janet Jackson, Mariah Carey, New Edition)
quote: The best productions have air in them. You've got to leave room for the listener to breathe. If every frequency is filled, the mix suffocates.
source: Sound On Sound — Jimmy Jam and Terry Lewis: The Sound of Minneapolis, April 2016

role: Producer (Janet Jackson, Alexander O'Neal, Usher)
quote: The rhythm section is everything. If the bass and drums don't make you move, no amount of production on top will fix it.
source: Sound On Sound — Jimmy Jam and Terry Lewis: The Sound of Minneapolis, April 2016

role: Producer (Jay-Z, Kanye West, Kendrick Lamar)
quote: Sample clearance changed my whole approach. Now I build sounds from scratch that feel sampled. The warmth of a sample comes from saturation and room tone.
source: Rolling Stone — Hit-Boy: Building From Scratch, November 2020

role: Producer (Vince Staples, Rico Nasty, Future)
quote: The snare is the personality of a beat. You can swap out everything else but if the snare is wrong, the whole thing feels wrong.
source: Genius — Open Mike: Kenny Beats on Production, 2020

role: Producer (Eminem, Drake, Kendrick Lamar)
quote: I'm always thinking about contrast. The verse has to be the opposite of the chorus sonically, not just lyrically.
source: Billboard — Boi-1da: The Craftsman, January 2018

role: Producer (Chris Brown, Beyoncé, Dr. Dre)
quote: Piano is my mixing board. I hear the arrangement at the keys before it ever gets into the computer.
source: Rolling Stone — Scott Storch: Back From The Brink, March 2019

role: Producer/Artist (Björk, FKA Twigs, Kanye West)
quote: Noise is just sound we haven't learned to love yet. When you process something until it becomes unrecognizable, you're creating a new instrument.
source: The Wire — Arca: Mutation, December 2015

role: Artist
quote: The production of a country record is about space and intimacy. Too much reverb and you lose the storytelling.
source: Sound On Sound — Shania Twain: Come On Over Sessions, March 1998

role: Recording/Mix Engineer (Jack White, Chris Stapleton, Buddy Guy)
quote: Tape saturation is not a plugin — it's a philosophy. When you push into tape you're making a commitment. Digital lets you undo everything. Tape forces decisions.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 116, 2016

role: Recording/Mix Engineer (Jack White, Chris Stapleton, Buddy Guy)
quote: Transients are the fingerprints of a performance. The way a drummer attacks a snare is unique to them. Compression that wipes out transients wipes out identity.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 116, 2016

role: Producer (U2, Peter Gabriel, Neil Young)
quote: Atmosphere is not an afterthought. Before I record a single note I want to know what the record smells like, what the light looks like in the room.
source: Soul Mining: A Musical Life — Daniel Lanois Memoir

role: Producer (U2, Peter Gabriel, Neil Young)
quote: Delay is a rhythmic conversation between the present and the recent past. Used musically, it's one of the most powerful tools in production.
source: Sound On Sound — Daniel Lanois: The Ambient Architect, September 2003

role: Producer (David Bowie, T. Rex, Morrissey)
quote: I gated John Bonham's drums on Heroes using noise gates triggered by the drums themselves. That's not just processing — it's composition.
source: Sound On Sound — The Making of Heroes, August 2012

role: Producer (David Bowie, T. Rex, Morrissey)
quote: Mid-side recording captures the center and the width as separate signals. When you process them independently you have surgical control over the stereo image.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 48, 2005

role: Electronics Designer (Neve consoles, Rupert Neve Designs)
quote: Transformers add harmonic richness that solid-state circuits can't replicate. The second and third harmonics they introduce are musically related to the fundamental. That's why they sound warm.
source: Sound On Sound — Rupert Neve: A Life In Audio, March 2008

role: Electronics Designer (Neve consoles, Rupert Neve Designs)
quote: The best equalizers add character when used aggressively. The worst ones just change level at a frequency. Great EQ has a musicality to it.
source: Sound On Sound — Rupert Neve: A Life In Audio, March 2008

role: Synthesizer Designer (Sequential Circuits, Prophet-5)
quote: The filter is the voice of the synthesizer. The oscillator gives it something to say — the filter determines how it says it.
source: Sound On Sound — Dave Smith: The Prophet's Prophet, October 2012

role: Drum Machine Designer (LinnDrum, MPC)
quote: Quantization removes the humanity from a performance. The MPC's swing is an attempt to give it back — to make the grid feel like a human played it.
source: Sound On Sound — Roger Linn: The Rhythm Machine, May 2009

role: Synthesizer Designer (Buchla Electric Music Box)
quote: Additive synthesis is telling the truth about sound. Every timbre in nature is a sum of sine waves. We're just making that explicit.
source: Sound On Sound — Don Buchla: The West Coast Vision, November 2007

role: Mastering Engineer (Michael Jackson, Prince, Carole King)
quote: Mastering is the last pass of the painter's eye. You're not changing the painting — you're making sure the colors are true.
source: Sound On Sound — Bernie Grundman: The Mastering Legend, February 2005

role: Mastering Engineer (Foo Fighters, Beck, Haim)
quote: The loudness war hurt music deeply. When everything is loud, nothing is loud. Dynamics are how music communicates emotion.
source: Sound On Sound — Emily Lazar: The Mastering Pioneer, June 2019

role: Mastering Engineer (Foo Fighters, Beck, Haim)
quote: I approach every master differently. The album tells me what it needs. Sometimes it needs warmth, sometimes clarity, sometimes just to get out of its own way.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 130, 2019

role: Mastering Engineer (Björk, Beach House, Slowdive)
quote: Mastering for streaming means understanding LUFS targets for every platform. What sounds right at -14 LUFS on Spotify sounds different on Apple Music at -16. You have to master for the ecosystem, not one platform.
source: Sound On Sound — Heba Kadry: Mastering for Streaming, March 2021

role: Mastering Engineer (Christine and the Queens, FKJ)
quote: True peak limiting is not optional in 2024. Inter-sample peaks cause clipping after encoding that you never hear in your DAW. It's an invisible problem that destroys streams.
source: Attack Magazine — Mastering for Streaming Platforms, 2022

role: Mix Engineer (U2, Frank Ocean, Florence + The Machine)
quote: Bus compression is the glue, but it should be invisible glue. If you can hear the bus compressor working, you're using too much.
source: Sound On Sound — Cenzo Townshend: The Art of the Mix Bus, April 2017

role: Mix Engineer (U2, Frank Ocean, Florence + The Machine)
quote: Send-return routing gives you control without commitment. The dry signal stays intact. You're blending in the effect, not replacing the source.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 118, 2016

role: Mix Engineer (Taylor Swift, Cardi B, Ariana Grande)
quote: Vocal compression on pop records is about consistency and presence, not dynamics control. The vocal has to cut through on earbuds and car speakers equally.
source: Sound On Sound — Serban Ghenea: Mixing Pop, January 2018

role: Mix Engineer (Taylor Swift, Cardi B, Ariana Grande)
quote: Modern pop mixes are deceptively simple. Everything feels effortless because an enormous amount of work went into making it feel that way.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 122, 2017

role: Mix Engineer (Jay-Z, Beyoncé, Kanye West)
quote: Hip-hop mixing is about the low end before anything else. If the 808 and the kick aren't locked, you have nothing to build on.
source: Sound On Sound — Young Guru: The Hip-Hop Mix, November 2011

role: Mix Engineer (Jay-Z, Beyoncé, Kanye West)
quote: Gain staging in hip-hop is about headroom for the 808. You need room for those sub frequencies to breathe or they clip the master bus before you even touch a limiter.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 99, 2013

role: Mix Engineer (Beyoncé, Justin Bieber, Chris Brown)
quote: Every mix I do starts with the low end. Get the kick and bass relationship right and the rest of the mix almost builds itself.
source: Sound On Sound — Jaycen Joshua: The Modern R&B Mix, August 2015

role: Producer/Engineer (Pearl Jam, Rage Against The Machine, Bruce Springsteen)
quote: Rock records live or die on the guitar tone. And guitar tone is saturation — the right amount of harmonic distortion in the right frequency range.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 29, 2002

role: Producer/Engineer (Pearl Jam, Rage Against The Machine, Bruce Springsteen)
quote: A great arrangement breathes. Verses that breathe make choruses that explode. Dynamic range is built into the structure of the song.
source: Sound On Sound — Brendan O'Brien: Rock Production, June 2008

role: Producer (Korn, Slipknot, At The Drive-In)
quote: I don't want the music to sound produced. I want it to sound dangerous. The minute you over-process something, you take the danger out of it.
source: Sound On Sound — Ross Robinson: The Brutal Truth, October 2001

role: Mix Engineer (Coldplay, John Mayer, Bob Dylan)
quote: I use multiple compressors in series on every channel. Each one does a small amount of work. Together they shape the dynamics in a way one compressor never could.
source: Sound On Sound — Michael Brauer: The Compressor Philosophy, March 2010

role: Mix Engineer (Coldplay, John Mayer, Bob Dylan)
quote: The mix bus is where the mix becomes a record. Everything upstream is preparation. The bus is the decision.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 82, 2011

role: Mix Engineer (Björk, Madonna, U2)
quote: I always ask myself — can I hear every element? If something is fighting for attention, something else has to move. Mixing is negotiation.
source: Sound On Sound — Spike Stent: The Art of Listening, July 2006

role: Mix Engineer (Björk, Madonna, U2)
quote: Chorus effect on a vocal at zero percent gives you a thickness that's impossible to achieve any other way. The listener doesn't know it's there — they just feel it.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 74, 2009

role: Mix Engineer (Foreigner, Timbaland, Aaliyah)
quote: The relationship between attack and release time on a compressor is more important than the ratio. Attack shapes the transient, release shapes the groove.
source: Sound On Sound — Jimmy Douglass: The Compressor Master, September 2007

role: Mix Engineer (Foreigner, Timbaland, Aaliyah)
quote: In R&B the vocal is not one element of the mix — it is the mix. Everything else is support structure for the voice.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 61, 2007

role: Producer/Engineer (Queens of the Stone Age, Good Charlotte, Smash Mouth)
quote: Tape delay has a musicality to it that digital delay struggles to match. The slight pitch variation as the tape speed fluctuates — that's not a flaw, it's the sound.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 51, 2005

role: Producer/Engineer (Kings of Leon, Tom Waits, Norah Jones)
quote: The attack on a compressor is the most important parameter to understand. Too fast and you kill the transient. Too slow and you're not really compressing at all.
source: Sound On Sound — Jacquire King: The Art of Compression, February 2015

role: Producer/Engineer (Kings of Leon, Tom Waits, Norah Jones)
quote: Reverb and delay working together can create depth that feels three-dimensional. The delay gives you width, the reverb gives you depth.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 87, 2011

role: Producer/Engineer (Beach House, TV on the Radio, Grizzly Bear)
quote: Plate reverb has a density that spring and room reverbs don't have. On vocals it creates a presence that pushes the singer forward even as the reverb expands behind them.
source: Sound On Sound — Chris Coady: Dream Pop Production, May 2016

role: Producer (Adele, Paul McCartney, Foo Fighters)
quote: The piano is the ultimate arrangement tool. It covers the full frequency range and forces you to think about voicing — which frequencies go where in the chord.
source: Sound On Sound — Greg Kurstin: The Pop Craftsman, October 2017

role: Producer/Engineer (St. Vincent, Sharon Van Etten, Angel Olsen)
quote: I think noise gates are used as a crutch. If your room sounds bad when the gate opens, the room sounds bad. Fix the room, not the gate.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 109, 2015

role: Mix Engineer (The Killers, Fleetwood Mac, Imagine Dragons)
quote: The snare and the vocal share the same frequency range. If you don't carve space for both, they fight each other on every beat.
source: Sound On Sound — Mark Needham: Frequency Management, December 2014

role: Mix Engineer (The Killers, Fleetwood Mac, Imagine Dragons)
quote: Automation on the mix bus in the final chorus — a tiny 0.5dB lift — is one of my most-used moves. The listener doesn't know it's there. They just feel the song grow.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 113, 2016

role: Mix Engineer (Steely Dan, Eagles, Fleetwood Mac)
quote: Steely Dan taught me that perfect is not enough. The recordings have to be perfect AND musical. Those two things are not automatically the same.
source: Sound On Sound — Elliot Scheiner: The Steely Dan Sessions, August 2003

role: Recording Engineer (Steely Dan)
quote: Digital audio is about precision. The sample rate and bit depth determine the resolution of your canvas. Work at the highest resolution you can and print down at the end.
source: Sound On Sound — Roger Nichols: The Digital Pioneer, June 2004

role: Producer/Engineer (Billy Joel, Paul Simon, Frank Sinatra)
quote: A great vocal recording requires three things: the right singer, the right room, and the right moment. You can't manufacture any of them.
source: Making Records: The Scenes Behind the Music — Phil Ramone Memoir

role: Producer/Engineer (Billy Joel, Paul Simon, Frank Sinatra)
quote: The arrangement has to have tension and release built into it. If every moment is equally important, no moment is important at all.
source: Making Records: The Scenes Behind the Music — Phil Ramone Memoir

role: Artist/Producer (Dead Can Dance)
quote: Reverb is not decoration. It is the acoustic truth of the performance. Strip it away and you strip away the sense of place.
source: Sound On Sound — Dead Can Dance: Into The Labyrinth Sessions, April 1993

role: Recording Engineer/Designer (Universal Audio founder)
quote: The console is only as good as the room it's in. I built studios before I built consoles because I knew the room was the instrument.
source: Sound On Sound — The Legacy of Bill Putnam, January 2009

role: Recording Engineer (Herbie Hancock, Bobby McFerrin, Kronos Quartet)
quote: Classical recording is about transparency. The job is to not be heard — to capture what's in the room without leaving fingerprints on it.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 40, 2004

role: Mix/Recording Engineer (Alicia Keys, Jay-Z, John Legend)
quote: Gain structure is the foundation of a great session. Every dB you manage correctly at the input saves you ten minutes of problem-solving at the mix.
source: Sound On Sound — Ann Mincieli: Engineering With Alicia Keys, September 2012

role: Producer/Engineer (Tool, System of a Down, Red Hot Chili Peppers)
quote: The noise gate is a blunt instrument but used creatively — gating a reverb return on a snare — it becomes something entirely different.
source: Recording Unhinged — Creative and Unconventional Music Recording Techniques

role: Mastering Engineer (Led Zeppelin, Daft Punk, Bruce Springsteen)
quote: The biggest mistake in mastering is trying to fix the mix. Mastering enhances what's there. It cannot replace what isn't.
source: Sound On Sound — Bob Ludwig: Mastering Legends, November 2015

role: Mastering Engineer (Led Zeppelin, Daft Punk, Bruce Springsteen)
quote: Dynamic range is not a technical specification — it's an emotional one. Loud and soft are how music creates meaning.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 104, 2014

role: Mix Engineer (Depeche Mode, Tears For Fears, Erasure)
quote: Synthesizer music lives and dies on the filter sweep. The cutoff frequency moving through a mix is as expressive as a guitarist bending a string.
source: Sound On Sound — Dave Bascombe: Mixing Electronic Music, July 2005

role: Producer (Björk, Depeche Mode, LFO)
quote: FM synthesis is brutal and beautiful at the same time. The feedback paths create harmonics that feel alive — almost biological.
source: Sound On Sound — LFO: Frequencies, March 1996

role: Producer (Public Enemy, LL Cool J)
quote: Sampling is not about finding a loop. It's about finding a feeling — a moment of sonic energy captured on tape — and recontextualizing it.
source: Sound On Sound — Public Enemy: Fear of a Black Planet Sessions, October 2000

role: Producer (Wu-Tang Clan, Ghostface Killah)
quote: I EQ out the bass before I sample. The original low end is replaced by my 808 and my kick. That's how you make an old record sit in a modern mix.
source: The Tao of Wu — RZA

role: Producer (Wu-Tang Clan, Ghostface Killah)
quote: The dirt in the sample — the hiss, the crackle, the noise — that's not something to remove. That's the humanity of the original recording coming through.
source: The Tao of Wu — RZA

role: Producer (A Tribe Called Quest, Erykah Badu, Common)
quote: I never quantize. The feel is in the imperfection. The moment you snap everything to the grid you lose the human element that makes it groove.
source: Scratch Magazine — J Dilla: The Beat Scientist, Issue 4, 2004

role: Producer (Jay-Z, Kendrick Lamar, Mary J. Blige)
quote: Soul music gives you something no synthesizer can — the feeling of a room full of musicians playing together in the same moment. Sampling captures that moment forever.
source: Sound On Sound — 9th Wonder: The Soul Digger, February 2010

role: Producer (Kendrick Lamar, MF DOOM, Erykah Badu)
quote: Lo-fi is not a mistake. It's a choice. The warmth in an old sample comes from the saturation and the compression of the original recording format.
source: Wax Poetics — Madlib: The Unseen, Issue 22, 2007

role: Producer (Jay-Z, Kanye West, Common)
quote: The kick drum frequency determines what kind of music you're making. 50Hz is a sub kick for electronic music. 80Hz is a hip-hop kick. 100Hz is rock. Know your genre, know your low end.
source: Billboard — No I.D.: The Architect of Sound, June 2016

role: Producer (Jay-Z, Beyoncé, DMX)
quote: Energy doesn't lie. If it doesn't move you when you play it back, it won't move anyone else either. Trust your gut before you trust your meters.
source: Rolling Stone — Swizz Beatz: The Energy Architect, April 2018

role: Producer (Jay-Z, Eminem, Kanye West)
quote: Sample-based production taught me that the arrangement is already in the original record. Your job is to find it and reveal it.
source: Sound On Sound — Just Blaze: The Blueprint Sessions, March 2012

role: Producer/Artist
quote: The 808 is the most important instrument in modern music. It's not a drum — it's a bass synthesizer that triggers on the beat.
source: Rolling Stone — Kanye West: 808s and Heartbreak Oral History, November 2018

role: Producer (Future, Drake, The Weeknd)
quote: The hi-hat pattern is the signature of a producer. Before you hear anything else, the hi-hat tells you who made the beat.
source: Billboard — Metro Boomin: The Signature Sound, September 2017

role: Producer (Drake, Nicki Minaj, Travis Scott)
quote: Trap production is all about the space between the notes. The silence in a trap beat is as important as the sounds. That's where the tension lives.
source: Complex — Murda Beatz: The Space Between, October 2019

role: Producer (EPMD, Redman, Keith Murray)
quote: I always pitched the sample down a step or two. Lower pitch means lower frequency content, which means more weight. That's where the bounce in a hip-hop record comes from.
source: Wax Poetics — EPMD: The Green-Eyed Bandit, Issue 18, 2006

role: Producer (CL Smooth, Nas, Kanye West)
quote: Chopping a sample is like editing film. Every cut changes the meaning. You're a director as much as a producer.
source: Wax Poetics — Pete Rock: The Soul Survivor, Issue 30, 2008

role: Producer (Gang Starr, Nas, Jay-Z)
quote: I want the sample to be unrecognizable but still feel familiar. You should feel the original in it without being able to name it.
source: Sound On Sound — DJ Premier: The Hard Knock Life, July 2001

role: Producer (Gang Starr, Nas, Jay-Z)
quote: The scratch is an instrument. It's not showing off — it's composition. Every scratch fill is a musical phrase.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 22, 2001

role: Producer/Artist (Run The Jewels, Company Flow)
quote: Distortion in hip-hop is emotional information. A heavily distorted bass says something that a clean bass cannot say.
source: Sound On Sound — El-P: Noise As Narrative, November 2014

role: Producer (Future, Miley Cyrus, Kendrick Lamar)
quote: I use a lot of space. The beat doesn't have to be full. Sometimes the most powerful thing is what you don't put in.
source: Rolling Stone — Mike Will Made-It: The Architecture of Trap, January 2014

role: Producer (Gucci Mane, Future, 21 Savage)
quote: Church music taught me dynamics. Gospel builds from quiet to explosive. Trap borrowed that emotional architecture.
source: Billboard — Zaytoven: From Church To Trap, March 2017

role: Producer (Future, Drake, Young Jeezy)
quote: Melody in a trap beat is underrated. The synth line or the flute — that's what makes a beat stick in your head after the drums stop.
source: Complex — Sonny Digital: The Melody Man, April 2016

role: Recording Engineer (Shania Twain, Faith Hill, Garth Brooks)
quote: Country recording is about capturing a performance in a room. The arrangement and the room are one thing — the mic placement is just choosing where to listen.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 45, 2005

role: Mix Engineer (Rush, Aerosmith, Dream Theater)
quote: In rock mixing, the kick and the bass guitar have to share the low end. One of them has to own the sub. Usually it's the kick. The bass fills in the upper lows.
source: Sound On Sound — Richard Chycki: Rock Mix Architecture, August 2016

role: Mix Engineer (Nirvana, Jeff Buckley, Rage Against The Machine)
quote: Nevermind had more reverb than people remember. Kurt's vocal had a plate on it that pushed it just slightly out of reality. That's what gives it the dreamlike quality.
source: Sound On Sound — The Making of Nevermind, September 2011

role: Mix/Recording Engineer (Alabama Shakes, Weezer, Kacey Musgraves)
quote: I embrace the ugly sounds. A distorted, clipped, wrong-sounding thing in the right place in a mix is worth more than ten perfect sounds in the wrong place.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 120, 2017

role: Mix/Recording Engineer (Alabama Shakes, Weezer, Kacey Musgraves)
quote: I often automate the dry-wet ratio of reverb through a song. The verb opens up in the chorus and contracts in the verse. The space breathes with the arrangement.
source: Sound On Sound — Shawn Everett: Unconventional Mixing, July 2019

role: Recording/Mix Engineer (Bob Dylan, Weezer, Public Enemy)
quote: Headroom in digital is free. There is no reason to track anywhere near 0dBFS. Leave yourself 12 to 18 dB and mix without limits.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 53, 2006

role: Mastering Engineer (Architects, Parkway Drive, Spiritbox)
quote: Metal mastering is about finding the ceiling that lets the dynamics breathe while still hitting the loudness targets for streaming. Too much limiting kills the aggression.
source: Attack Magazine — Mastering Metal in the Streaming Era, 2021

role: Mix/Mastering Engineer (Opeth, Amon Amarth, Paradise Lost)
quote: In metal mixing, the kick drum is a wall of energy not a single impact. It requires compression, multiband shaping, and sub EQ all working together.
source: Sound On Sound — Jens Bogren: The Metal Mix, April 2018

role: Mix Engineer (Michael Jackson — Invincible)
quote: On Invincible we used parallel compression on the drum bus set to extreme ratios — 20:1 — and blended it at maybe 15 percent. That's what gives those drums their inhuman power.
source: Sound On Sound — The Making of Invincible, March 2002

role: Recording Engineer (Michael Jackson, Quincy Jones, Count Basie)
quote: The sound of Thriller is the sound of a great room. Studio B at Westlake had a live end, a dead end, and the ability to make anything sound like it belonged in the same space.
source: In The Studio With Michael Jackson — Bruce Swedien

role: Recording Engineer (Michael Jackson, Quincy Jones, Count Basie)
quote: The human voice is the most complex instrument to record because it carries emotion that no microphone can fully capture. Your job is to get as close to that truth as possible.
source: In The Studio With Michael Jackson — Bruce Swedien

role: Artists/Producers
quote: We treat synthesizers the way other bands treat guitars — as instruments that respond to the player. The same patch played differently sounds like a different instrument.
source: Sound On Sound — Underworld: Born Slippy Sessions, November 1995

role: Producer/Guitarist (David Bowie, Daft Punk, Madonna)
quote: Funk guitar is about what you don't play. The rhythm is in the silence between the notes, and the tone is in the upper mid frequencies where the cut happens.
source: Le Freak: An Upside Down Story of Family, Disco, and Destiny — Nile Rodgers

role: Producer/Guitarist (David Bowie, Daft Punk, Madonna)
quote: Get Lucky was built on a foundation of extreme simplicity. Four chords, a groove, and the best musicians in the world playing less than they were capable of. That restraint is what made it a hit.
source: Rolling Stone — Nile Rodgers: The Craft of Simplicity, March 2014

role: Producer (Donna Summer, Daft Punk, Blondie)
quote: The synthesizer bass changed everything. Before that, bass was something a human played with feel and imperfection. The synthesizer bass was perfect and relentless — that's what made it dance.
source: Sound On Sound — Giorgio Moroder: The Father of Disco, February 2013

role: Artist/Producer
quote: We are not making music about machines. We are making music with machines. The synthesizer is the instrument of our time the way the piano was the instrument of the 19th century.
source: Sound On Sound — Kraftwerk: The Computer World Sessions, May 2003

role: Artist/Producer
quote: The vocoder is a conversation between the voice and the synthesizer. Neither one leads — they merge into something that is neither human nor machine.
source: Sound On Sound — Herbie Hancock: Future Shock Sessions, June 1999

role: Composer/Producer (Switched-On Bach, A Clockwork Orange)
quote: The synthesizer is not an imitation of acoustic instruments — it is a new family of instruments entirely. The moment you accept that, you stop trying to make it sound like something else.
source: Sound On Sound — Wendy Carlos: Switched-On Legacy, October 2005

role: Film Composer/Producer
quote: In film scoring, silence is the most powerful dynamic tool. The contrast between full orchestral swell and complete silence is where the emotion lives.
source: Sound On Sound — Hans Zimmer: The Score, December 2014

role: Film Composer/Producer
quote: I synthesize orchestral sounds not to replace orchestras but to find the sounds in between — the sounds that don't exist in the acoustic world.
source: MasterClass — Hans Zimmer Teaches Film Scoring

role: Film Composer
quote: Reverb is memory. When I compose for film I think about the acoustic memory of the scene — the space the characters occupy. The reverb tells you where you are.
source: Sound On Sound — Ennio Morricone: The Art of Film Music, September 2001

role: Film Composer/Producer (Mad Max: Fury Road, Batman v Superman)
quote: Sidechaining the orchestra to a kick drum is insane on paper. But in a film action sequence, the rhythm created by that sidechain pumping is exactly what the scene needs.
source: Sound On Sound — Junkie XL: Action Scoring, March 2016

role: Producer (Frankie Goes to Hollywood, Pet Shop Boys, Seal)
quote: Production is about creating a world the listener inhabits for three and a half minutes. Every element — the compression, the reverb, the arrangement — is part of that world's architecture.
source: Sound On Sound — Trevor Horn: The Art of Production, January 2010

role: Producer (Frankie Goes to Hollywood, Pet Shop Boys, Seal)
quote: Relax was recorded and mixed eight times before we were happy. Each mix taught us something about what the song needed to be. Sometimes the process is the point.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 93, 2012

role: Producer (Kylie Minogue, Rick Astley, Bananarama)
quote: A hit record needs to work on a transistor radio. If it doesn't translate to the worst possible playback system, the mix isn't done.
source: Sound On Sound — Stock Aitken Waterman: The Hit Factory, June 2003

role: Producer/Songwriter (Pink, Christina Aguilera, Gwen Stefani)
quote: A vocal performance is not a technical achievement. It is an emotional communication. When you over-produce a vocal you communicate less, not more.
source: Rolling Stone — Linda Perry: The Producer Who Listens, September 2012

role: Mix Engineer (Alice in Chains, Metallica, KISS)
quote: Heavy music has to be heavy in the low mids as well as the lows. Sub bass alone is not weight. The 200-400Hz range is where you feel the chest compression.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 68, 2008

role: Mix Engineer (Slipknot, Machine Head, Trivium)
quote: Metal guitar tones require surgical EQ. The 400Hz mud has to go, the 3kHz presence has to come up, and you need to decide where the high end lives without the cymbals fighting it.
source: Sound On Sound — Colin Richardson: The Metal Mix, October 2006

role: Producer/Engineer (Judas Priest, Thin Lizzy, Gary Moore)
quote: I invented the Lunchbox ambient miking technique by accident. I put a mic in a biscuit tin in the corridor and the reverb it produced was exactly what the snare needed.
source: Sound On Sound — Chris Tsangarides: The Heavy Metal Engineer, May 2001

role: Producer/Engineer (King Crimson, Ulver, Astra)
quote: The biggest mistake in modern production is mistaking loudness for power. Power is dynamic range. When the loud parts are really loud because the quiet parts are really quiet — that is power.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 85, 2011

role: Mix/Recording Engineer (Queens of the Stone Age, Bad Religion, Tool)
quote: I use saturation on almost everything. Not to distort it — to warm it up and give it harmonic richness that sits better in the mix.
source: Sound On Sound — Joe Barresi: The Analog Mindset, September 2014

role: Producer/Artist
quote: The filter resonance at the right frequency will make a room physically respond. That's not a metaphor — it's physics. The right resonant peak in a sub system creates a standing wave.
source: Resident Advisor — Daniel Avery: The Physics of Dance Music, March 2017

role: Producer/Artist (Plastikman)
quote: Minimal techno is compression taken to its logical extreme. You strip everything that isn't essential until what remains feels inevitable.
source: Resident Advisor — Richie Hawtin: The Minimal Manifesto, June 2012

role: Producer/Artist (The Wizard)
quote: A drum machine is an instrument. When I play the 909 live I'm performing on it the same way a drummer performs on a kit. The machine responds to touch.
source: Resident Advisor — Jeff Mills: The Machine Operator, September 2009

role: Producer/Artist
quote: I use compression on everything — not to control dynamics but to add color. Different compressors have different characters. A 1176 on a drum bus sounds nothing like an LA-2A.
source: Sound On Sound — Moby: The Compressor Collection, July 2008

role: Producer/Artist (NSYNC, Madonna, Peter Gabriel)
quote: Granular synthesis takes a sound and atomizes it into thousands of tiny grains. When you reassemble those grains at different speeds and pitches, you create sounds that have never existed.
source: Sound On Sound — BT: The Stutter Edit Pioneer, February 2011

role: Producer/Artist
quote: Drum and bass is an exercise in extreme compression and extreme dynamics simultaneously. The kick can destroy a speaker system but the hi-hats have to be delicate.
source: Sound On Sound — Photek: The Drum Scientist, August 1997

role: Producer/Artist (Metalheadz)
quote: The Amen break has been compressed, time-stretched, pitched, reversed, gated, and destroyed in every possible way. And it still sounds like the Amen break. That's what makes a great sample.
source: Sound On Sound — Goldie: Timeless Sessions, October 1995

role: Producer/Artist
quote: I compress things until they feel broken. Then I add noise and vinyl crackle until they feel like a memory. The lo-fi quality is not an accident — it's how the music feels.
source: The Wire — Burial: The Untold Story, February 2008

role: Producer/Artist
quote: I process vocals until they become another texture in the arrangement, not just the lead element. The voice is an instrument like any other — it can be stretched, compressed, destroyed.
source: Sound On Sound — James Blake: Voice As Instrument, April 2013

role: Producer (The xx, Rihanna, Florence + The Machine)
quote: Club music has to work at 110dB through a d&b system in a room with terrible acoustics. The mix has to be so good it survives that environment.
source: Resident Advisor — Jamie xx: Mixing For The Club, November 2015

role: Producer/Artist
quote: I studied neuroscience alongside music production. The way frequency affects the nervous system directly — that's not metaphor. Certain frequencies physically change how you feel.
source: Sound On Sound — Floating Points: Science of Sound, January 2019

role: Producer/Artist
quote: Reverb is not space — it's time. Every reverb tail is a measurement of duration. When I use reverb I'm thinking about how long I want the listener to live in that moment.
source: Resident Advisor — Nicolas Jaar: Time and Space, August 2016

role: Composer/Producer
quote: I often process acoustic recordings through synthesizers and vice versa. The boundary between acoustic and electronic should be porous. That's where the interesting sounds live.
source: Sound On Sound — Max Richter: Recomposing Vivaldi, March 2012

role: Producer/Artist
quote: Synthesis is archaeology of the future. You're finding sounds that don't exist yet by manipulating the parameters of what does exist.
source: The Wire — Oneohtrix Point Never: Garden of Delete, November 2015

role: Producer/Artist
quote: I want my music to sound like it's malfunctioning. Compression that pumps incorrectly, filters that resonate at the wrong frequency — the errors become the aesthetic.
source: Resident Advisor — Actress: Beautiful Errors, May 2014

role: Producer/Artist
quote: The bass guitar through a distortion pedal into a DI is the most extreme saturation device I know. The harmonic content it generates is something synthesis cannot fully replicate.
source: Sound On Sound — Squarepusher: Bass Extremist, July 2004

role: Producer/Artist
quote: I built my own synthesizers because commercial ones didn't do what I heard in my head. The synthesis I wanted didn't exist, so I had to make it exist.
source: Sound On Sound — Aphex Twin: The Custom Hardware Sessions, April 2014

role: Producers/Artists
quote: We use compression as a compositional tool — not to control levels but to create rhythmic artifacts. The pumping and breathing of an overworked compressor is a musical event.
source: The Wire — Autechre: Anti EP and Beyond, March 1994

role: Producers/Artists
quote: Chorus gives a synth warmth that pure digital synthesis lacks. We often layer a clean synth with a heavily chorused version at low volume. You don't hear the chorus — you feel the width.
source: Sound On Sound — Röyksopp: Melody AM Sessions, September 2002

role: Producer/Artist (Brian Eno, Stormzy, Headie One)
quote: I record everything — voice memos, ambient sounds, conversations. The raw material for music is everywhere. The production is just choosing what to keep.
source: NME — Fred Again: Everything Is Music, June 2022

role: Producer/Artist
quote: Sampling from your own recordings is the most honest form of sampling. You made the original sound — now you're recontextualizing your own creative decisions.
source: Resident Advisor — Four Tet: The Long Game, March 2020

role: Producers/Artists
quote: Multiband compression on a mix bus is a surgical tool. You compress the low end differently from the mids differently from the highs. The result is a master that feels balanced at any volume.
source: Sound On Sound — Soulwax: The Production Philosophy, February 2018

role: Producer/Artist
quote: In dance music, the drop is a dynamic event not a sonic one. You can drop to almost silence and it hits harder than anything loud. Dynamic range is the weapon.
source: Resident Advisor — Tiga: The Drop Scientist, October 2013

role: Producer/DJ (Innervisions)
quote: The low end in a club is a physical experience. When I EQ for a club system I'm thinking about the standing waves in the room, not just the frequency response of my monitors.
source: Resident Advisor — Dixon: Sound System Philosophy, April 2016

role: Producers/DJs
quote: We always reference our mixes on a club system before we call them done. What sounds right in the studio sounds completely different when the sub frequencies interact with a room.
source: Resident Advisor — Âme: From Studio to System, January 2017

role: Producer/Artist
quote: I treat the mix bus compressor like an instrument. Different settings create different grooves — a fast release on a slow track creates a lift that no other technique can.
source: Sound On Sound — Henrik Schwarz: The Mix Bus Sessions, July 2015

role: Producer/Artist
quote: I have a rule: every sound in my music must have been recorded, not synthesized. The microphone is the instrument. Sampling reality is more interesting than sampling other music.
source: The Wire — Matthew Herbert: The Recording Rule, September 2005

role: Producer/Artist
quote: I compress and distort acoustic recordings until they sound electronic, and I make electronic sounds try to feel acoustic. The border between the two is the interesting place.
source: Sound On Sound — Caribou: Swim Sessions, May 2010

role: Producer/Artist (Border Community)
quote: Analog modular synthesis is not about control — it's about relinquishing it. The patching creates feedback loops that produce musical results you could never program deliberately.
source: Resident Advisor — James Holden: The Inheritors Sessions, October 2013

role: Producer/Artist
quote: Noise gating a reverb tail at a musical threshold creates a rhythmic element that no quantized sequencer can replicate. The gate becomes the rhythm.
source: Sound On Sound — Actress: Ghettoville Sessions, February 2014

role: Producer/Artist
quote: FM synthesis produces inharmonic partials that feel unstable and alive. That instability is what makes it emotional — it sounds like something is being born or dying.
source: Resident Advisor — Max Cooper: Generative Music, December 2018

role: Producer/Artist (Warp Records)
quote: I use distortion as a dynamic tool not a tonal one. Automating the drive parameter through a distortion plugin creates movement that compression alone can't generate.
source: Sound On Sound — Clark: Body Riddle Sessions, March 2006

role: Producer/Artist
quote: I mix at low volumes deliberately. Everything that sounds good at low volume sounds incredible loud. Everything that only sounds good loud is compensating for something missing.
source: Sound On Sound — Bonobo: Migration Sessions, January 2017

role: Producer (Everything But The Girl, Martha Wash)
quote: House music is built on the four-on-the-floor kick. That kick has to be felt in the chest at club volume. Everything else in the mix is in service of that kick.
source: Sound On Sound — Todd Terry: The House Blueprint, April 1998

role: Producer (Mr. Fingers)
quote: Deep house is about the feeling underneath the sound. The sub bass frequencies create an emotional resonance that bypasses the conscious mind entirely.
source: Red Bull Music Academy — Larry Heard: The Deep House Lectures, 2014

role: Producer/DJ (The Warehouse, Chicago)
quote: The Roland TR-909 kick drum was the sound of Chicago. Not because it was the best kick drum — because it had the right frequency response for the club sound systems we were using.
source: Sound On Sound — Frankie Knuckles: The Birth of House, March 2004

role: Drummer/Producer (Fela Kuti, Damon Albarn)
quote: Afrobeat has no single rhythm. It is the conversation between many rhythms happening simultaneously. The drumkit is an orchestra, not an instrument.
source: Sound On Sound — Tony Allen: The Afrobeat Architect, November 2009

role: Drummer/Producer (Keith Richards, John Mayer, The Vampire Weekend)
quote: The snare is the heartbeat of a rock record. Every other element breathes in and out around it. Get the snare wrong and the whole body of music is sick.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 100, 2014

role: Session Drummer (Aretha Franklin, Steely Dan, James Brown)
quote: The Purdie Shuffle is compression on the drumkit. The ghost notes fill the space and create a density that makes the downbeat hit harder by contrast.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 67, 2008

role: Session Drummer (James Brown)
quote: The Funky Drummer beat is about the space between the notes. I was playing what wasn't there as much as what was.
source: Modern Drummer — Clyde Stubblefield Interview, August 2007

role: Drummer (The Police)
quote: The snare and the hi-hat in reggae exist in a different time dimension from the bass. The off-beat is not an afterthought — it is the architecture.
source: Sound On Sound — Stewart Copeland: The Police Rhythms, June 2008

role: Session Drummer (The Wrecking Crew)
quote: I played on 40 number one records and I can tell you the secret: play what the song needs, not what you can do. Less is always more.
source: Wrecking Crew: The Inside Story of Rock and Roll's Best-Kept Secret — Kent Hartman

role: Producer (Johnny Cash, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Adele)
quote: To make something great, you have to be willing to make something terrible. The willingness to fail is the prerequisite for the possibility of great work.
source: The Creative Act: A Way of Being — Rick Rubin (2023)

role: Producer (Johnny Cash, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Adele)
quote: Finishing a work is a decision, not an achievement. At some point you decide it is done. Nothing external tells you when that is.
source: The Creative Act: A Way of Being — Rick Rubin (2023)

role: Producer (Johnny Cash, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Adele)
quote: The best work often comes when you are the least certain. Certainty can be the enemy of discovery.
source: The Creative Act: A Way of Being — Rick Rubin (2023)

role: Producer (Johnny Cash, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Adele)
quote: We are not trying to make a record that sounds like what is already out there. We are trying to make a record that sounds like itself.
source: The Creative Act: A Way of Being — Rick Rubin (2023)

role: Producer (Johnny Cash, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Adele)
quote: The details matter enormously. Not because the listener will consciously notice them, but because they will feel the difference between a work where the details were considered and one where they were not.
source: The Creative Act: A Way of Being — Rick Rubin (2023)

role: Producer (Johnny Cash, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Adele)
quote: Trying to make a hit record is the surest way to not make one. The goal is to make something that matters to you. If it matters to you, it has a chance of mattering to someone else.
source: The Creative Act: A Way of Being — Rick Rubin (2023)

role: Producer (Johnny Cash, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Adele)
quote: Simplicity is the most difficult thing to achieve. It takes more courage to strip something down than to add to it.
source: The Creative Act: A Way of Being — Rick Rubin (2023)

role: Producer (Johnny Cash, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Adele)
quote: Rules in art are not laws. They are patterns that have worked before. The question is always whether they work here, for this specific piece, right now.
source: The Creative Act: A Way of Being — Rick Rubin (2023)

role: Producer (Johnny Cash, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Adele)
quote: When something isn't working, the solution is rarely to do more of it. More often it is to step back and question the premise.
source: The Creative Act: A Way of Being — Rick Rubin (2023)

role: Producer (Johnny Cash, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Adele)
quote: The artist's job is not to judge the work but to make it. Judgment belongs to a later stage. Mixing the two is how creative blocks are born.
source: The Creative Act: A Way of Being — Rick Rubin (2023)

role: Producer (Johnny Cash, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Adele)
quote: Every artist has taste. Taste is what you respond to. Craft is the ability to make what your taste demands. The gap between the two is where you live until you close it.
source: The Creative Act: A Way of Being — Rick Rubin (2023)

role: Producer (Johnny Cash, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Adele)
quote: Collaboration works when each person brings their full self and is willing to let go of the outcome. The ego that needs credit is the enemy of the work.
source: The Creative Act: A Way of Being — Rick Rubin (2023)

role: Mastering Engineer — author of Mastering Audio
quote: Every dB of gain reduction applied at mastering is a dB of dynamic range permanently removed from the music. This is not a reversible process.
source: Mastering Audio: The Art and the Science — Bob Katz (Third Edition)

role: Mastering Engineer — author of Mastering Audio
quote: The K-System was designed to give mixers and mastering engineers a common reference point. When everyone calibrates to the same monitor level, loudness decisions become meaningful again.
source: Mastering Audio: The Art and the Science — Bob Katz (Third Edition)

role: Mastering Engineer — author of Mastering Audio
quote: Inter-sample peaks are not theoretical. They are real distortion that occurs after the DAC when audio is reconstructed. A true peak limiter is the only defense.
source: Mastering Audio: The Art and the Science — Bob Katz (Third Edition)

role: Mastering Engineer — author of Mastering Audio
quote: Dither is not optional when reducing bit depth. Without it, low-level signals are quantized to silence or distorted. Dither adds a small amount of shaped noise that preserves those signals.
source: Mastering Audio: The Art and the Science — Bob Katz (Third Edition)

role: Mastering Engineer — author of Mastering Audio
quote: A mastering EQ should be used in increments of tenths of a dB, not whole numbers. At mastering, half a dB is a large move. A full dB is a statement.
source: Mastering Audio: The Art and the Science — Bob Katz (Third Edition)

role: Mastering Engineer — author of Mastering Audio
quote: The most common mastering mistake is over-compression. A mix that breathes will always translate better than one that has been squeezed into a narrow dynamic window.
source: Mastering Audio: The Art and the Science — Bob Katz (Third Edition)

role: Mastering Engineer — author of Mastering Audio
quote: Reference tracks are not targets — they are calibration tools. You are not trying to sound like the reference. You are trying to understand how your monitoring compares to a known quantity.
source: Mastering Audio: The Art and the Science — Bob Katz (Third Edition)

role: Mastering Engineer — author of Mastering Audio
quote: Stereo width in mastering must be approached carefully. Mono compatibility is not optional — a mix that collapses to mono has lost half its potential audience.
source: Mastering Audio: The Art and the Science — Bob Katz (Third Edition)

role: Mastering Engineer — author of Mastering Audio
quote: The ear adapts to loudness within seconds. This is why A/B comparisons at different levels are meaningless. Always match levels before comparing.
source: Mastering Audio: The Art and the Science — Bob Katz (Third Edition)

role: Mastering Engineer — author of Mastering Audio
quote: The low end below 40Hz is rarely musical. High-passing the master at 20-30Hz removes inaudible content that wastes headroom and causes inter-sample peaks.
source: Mastering Audio: The Art and the Science — Bob Katz (Third Edition)

role: Recording Engineer (The Beatles — Revolver, Sgt. Pepper's, Abbey Road)
quote: Norman Smith told me the rules and I promptly broke most of them. Not out of disrespect — out of curiosity. I wanted to know what happened if you did the thing you weren't supposed to do.
source: Here, There and Everywhere: My Life Recording the Music of the Beatles — Geoff Emerick (2006)

role: Recording Engineer (The Beatles — Revolver, Sgt. Pepper's, Abbey Road)
quote: The sound of Ringo's drums on Revolver came from putting the microphone inside the bass drum. EMI said you couldn't do that. We did it anyway and it changed the sound of popular music.
source: Here, There and Everywhere: My Life Recording the Music of the Beatles — Geoff Emerick (2006)

role: Recording Engineer (The Beatles — Revolver, Sgt. Pepper's, Abbey Road)
quote: Paul's bass on Revolver was recorded direct into the desk. No amp, no room, just the instrument into the board. It gave a clarity and presence that had never been on a pop record before.
source: Here, There and Everywhere: My Life Recording the Music of the Beatles — Geoff Emerick (2006)

role: Recording Engineer (The Beatles — Revolver, Sgt. Pepper's, Abbey Road)
quote: John wanted his voice to sound like it was coming from the moon. We put it through a rotating Leslie speaker cabinet. The result was Tomorrow Never Knows.
source: Here, There and Everywhere: My Life Recording the Music of the Beatles — Geoff Emerick (2006)

role: Recording Engineer (The Beatles — Revolver, Sgt. Pepper's, Abbey Road)
quote: We used the recording console as a compositional tool. The ADT — Artificial Double Tracking — was invented specifically because John hated listening to his own voice and didn't want to double-track everything.
source: Here, There and Everywhere: My Life Recording the Music of the Beatles — Geoff Emerick (2006)

role: Recording Engineer (The Beatles — Revolver, Sgt. Pepper's, Abbey Road)
quote: Sgt. Pepper's was made on four tracks. What we accomplished on four tracks by bouncing and careful planning taught me that limitations force creativity in ways that unlimited tracks never do.
source: Here, There and Everywhere: My Life Recording the Music of the Beatles — Geoff Emerick (2006)

role: Recording Engineer (The Beatles — Revolver, Sgt. Pepper's, Abbey Road)
quote: The sound of Abbey Road is the sound of that building. The echo chamber, the stairwell, the live room — you cannot separate the music from the architecture it was recorded in.
source: Here, There and Everywhere: My Life Recording the Music of the Beatles — Geoff Emerick (2006)

role: Recording Engineer (The Beatles — Revolver, Sgt. Pepper's, Abbey Road)
quote: I always thought of the compressor as a shaping tool rather than a control tool. Shaping the envelope of a sound — the way it breathes and decays — is a musical decision.
source: Here, There and Everywhere: My Life Recording the Music of the Beatles — Geoff Emerick (2006)

role: Recording Engineer (The Beatles — Revolver, Sgt. Pepper's, Abbey Road)
quote: George Martin and I communicated without words much of the time. He would raise an eyebrow and I would understand that something needed to change. That kind of listening is what makes a great session.
source: Here, There and Everywhere: My Life Recording the Music of the Beatles — Geoff Emerick (2006)

role: Recording Engineer (The Beatles — Revolver, Sgt. Pepper's, Abbey Road)
quote: Close-miking the brass on Penny Lane gave it that bright, almost painful presence. We were violating every rule of orchestral recording. But that sound defined the record.
source: Here, There and Everywhere: My Life Recording the Music of the Beatles — Geoff Emerick (2006)

role: Producer/Engineer (Billy Joel, Paul Simon, Frank Sinatra)
quote: I always tell singers: the microphone is not a barrier between you and the listener. It is a window. Sing through it, not into it.
source: Making Records: The Scenes Behind the Music — Phil Ramone (2007)

role: Producer/Engineer (Billy Joel, Paul Simon, Frank Sinatra)
quote: Frank Sinatra could walk into a studio, do one take, and leave. Not because he was lazy — because he had rehearsed so thoroughly that the first take was always the performance.
source: Making Records: The Scenes Behind the Music — Phil Ramone (2007)

role: Producer/Engineer (Billy Joel, Paul Simon, Frank Sinatra)
quote: Billy Joel writes at the piano and that's where his records have to start. The piano is not an accompaniment instrument for him — it is the architecture of the song.
source: Making Records: The Scenes Behind the Music — Phil Ramone (2007)

role: Producer/Engineer (Billy Joel, Paul Simon, Frank Sinatra)
quote: The studio is a psychological environment as much as a technical one. The engineer's job is to make the artist feel safe enough to take risks. Without that safety, you get performances, not revelations.
source: Making Records: The Scenes Behind the Music — Phil Ramone (2007)

role: Producer/Engineer (Billy Joel, Paul Simon, Frank Sinatra)
quote: Paul Simon hears counterpoint in everything. His arrangements work because every voice — every instrument — has its own melodic identity. Nothing is just filling space.
source: Making Records: The Scenes Behind the Music — Phil Ramone (2007)

role: Producer/Engineer (Billy Joel, Paul Simon, Frank Sinatra)
quote: I believe in capturing the moment. The headphones off, the lights low, the artist barely aware the tape is running — that's when the real performance happens.
source: Making Records: The Scenes Behind the Music — Phil Ramone (2007)

role: Producer/Engineer (Billy Joel, Paul Simon, Frank Sinatra)
quote: The mix is a conversation between what the artist intended and what the listener needs. Sometimes those are the same thing. Often they are not. The producer lives in that gap.
source: Making Records: The Scenes Behind the Music — Phil Ramone (2007)

role: Producer/Engineer (Billy Joel, Paul Simon, Frank Sinatra)
quote: A great ballad needs space. Every note has to breathe. If the arrangement is too dense the emotion gets trapped inside it and never reaches the listener.
source: Making Records: The Scenes Behind the Music — Phil Ramone (2007)

role: Producer (U2, Peter Gabriel, Neil Young, Bob Dylan)
quote: I build sounds from the ground up in the studio. I don't go in with a plan. I go in with instruments and I let the sounds tell me what the song wants to be.
source: Soul Mining: A Musical Life — Daniel Lanois (2010)

role: Producer (U2, Peter Gabriel, Neil Young, Bob Dylan)
quote: The Eno approach changed my life: treat the studio as an instrument, not a documentation device. You are not recording a band — you are composing a record.
source: Soul Mining: A Musical Life — Daniel Lanois (2010)

role: Producer (U2, Peter Gabriel, Neil Young, Bob Dylan)
quote: Bono and I would work for hours on a single chord. Not the melody, not the lyric — the chord. Because the emotional content of the record lives in the harmony.
source: Soul Mining: A Musical Life — Daniel Lanois (2010)

role: Producer (U2, Peter Gabriel, Neil Young, Bob Dylan)
quote: I love the sound of a room that has been played in for a long time. The acoustic memory of a space — the way it absorbs and reflects — is a living thing.
source: Soul Mining: A Musical Life — Daniel Lanois (2010)

role: Producer (U2, Peter Gabriel, Neil Young, Bob Dylan)
quote: The pedal steel guitar is one of the most emotional instruments ever built. The way it sustains and bends — it sounds like the human voice at its most vulnerable.
source: Soul Mining: A Musical Life — Daniel Lanois (2010)

role: Producer (U2, Peter Gabriel, Neil Young, Bob Dylan)
quote: Oh Mercy with Bob Dylan was recorded in a house in New Orleans. The ambience of that building — the humidity, the wooden floors, the street sounds — became part of the record.
source: Soul Mining: A Musical Life — Daniel Lanois (2010)

role: Producer (U2, Peter Gabriel, Neil Young, Bob Dylan)
quote: Brian Eno taught me that you can make a decision and then make a better decision. The first decision opens the door. It does not have to be the final one.
source: Soul Mining: A Musical Life — Daniel Lanois (2010)

role: Recording Engineer (David Bowie, The Beatles, Elton John, Supertramp)
quote: Working with The Beatles in the early days meant learning to anticipate what they needed before they knew they needed it. The engineer has to be invisible but always present.
source: Abbey Road to Ziggy Stardust — Ken Scott (2012)

role: Recording Engineer (David Bowie, The Beatles, Elton John, Supertramp)
quote: Ziggy Stardust was made with deliberate sonic violence. We wanted the guitars to sound like they were tearing through the speakers. That aggression was a production decision, not an accident.
source: Abbey Road to Ziggy Stardust — Ken Scott (2012)

role: Recording Engineer (David Bowie, The Beatles, Elton John, Supertramp)
quote: David Bowie was the most collaborative artist I ever worked with. He had a vision but he held it loosely — he was always willing to let an accident become the direction.
source: Abbey Road to Ziggy Stardust — Ken Scott (2012)

role: Recording Engineer (David Bowie, The Beatles, Elton John, Supertramp)
quote: Elton John and Bernie Taupin worked so fast it was almost frightening. The demos were close to finished records. The arrangement was in the song before we even entered the studio.
