source: Abbey Road to Ziggy Stardust — Ken Scott (2012)

role: Recording Engineer (David Bowie, The Beatles, Elton John, Supertramp)
quote: I learned from George Martin that the producer's job is not to impose — it is to reveal. You reveal what is already in the artist and the song.
source: Abbey Road to Ziggy Stardust — Ken Scott (2012)

role: Producer/Engineer (Tool, System of a Down, Prince, Johnny Cash)
quote: I once recorded a vocal through a garden hose into a tin can. It sounded exactly like the song needed. The strangest path often leads to the right destination.
source: Recording Unhinged — Sylvia Massy (2016)

role: Producer/Engineer (Tool, System of a Down, Prince, Johnny Cash)
quote: I believe every sound in the universe has been recorded somewhere already. Your job as an engineer is to find it, not to manufacture it.
source: Recording Unhinged — Sylvia Massy (2016)

role: Producer/Engineer (Tool, System of a Down, Prince, Johnny Cash)
quote: I put a microphone at the end of a long tube, dropped it into a lake, and recorded the sound of underwater pressure. That became a kick drum on a Tool demo.
source: Recording Unhinged — Sylvia Massy (2016)

role: Producer/Engineer (Tool, System of a Down, Prince, Johnny Cash)
quote: Extreme EQ is a valid creative tool. Boosting 10dB at a single frequency is not wrong if it sounds right. Rules about EQ amounts assume you are trying to sound normal.
source: Recording Unhinged — Sylvia Massy (2016)

role: Producer/Engineer (Tool, System of a Down, Prince, Johnny Cash)
quote: I have a collection of found objects I use as percussion. A rusted chain, a piece of sheet metal, a broken spring. The world is full of instruments that have never been named.
source: Recording Unhinged — Sylvia Massy (2016)

role: Producer/Engineer (Tool, System of a Down, Prince, Johnny Cash)
quote: When a session is going badly I stop and make everyone laugh. A laugh resets the nervous system. You cannot get a great performance from a tense body.
source: Recording Unhinged — Sylvia Massy (2016)

role: Author — Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio
quote: The single most important thing you can do for your mix is fix your monitoring environment. You cannot make good decisions about sound in a room that lies to you.
source: Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio — Mike Senior (Second Edition)

role: Author — Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio
quote: Small room acoustics create comb filtering and standing waves that make certain frequencies louder or quieter than they actually are. EQ decisions made in an untreated room are unreliable.
source: Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio — Mike Senior (Second Edition)

role: Author — Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio
quote: Low-budget nearfield monitors are often more reliable than expensive speakers in an untreated room. A small speaker in a good position tells fewer lies than a large speaker in a bad one.
source: Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio — Mike Senior (Second Edition)

role: Author — Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio
quote: Checking your mix on multiple playback systems is not optional. The mix that sounds good on studio monitors, earbuds, a laptop speaker, and a car stereo is a finished mix.
source: Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio — Mike Senior (Second Edition)

role: Author — Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio
quote: The low-mid buildup between 200-500Hz is the enemy of clarity in small room mixes. Most amateur mixes sound muddy because this region is boosted by the room and never corrected.
source: Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio — Mike Senior (Second Edition)

role: Author — Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio
quote: Automation is not a finishing step — it is a fundamental part of mixing. A mix without automation is a static photograph of a dynamic performance.
source: Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio — Mike Senior (Second Edition)

role: Author — Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio
quote: Phase relationships between microphones are one of the most overlooked elements of mixing. Two mics on the same source can cancel each other at certain frequencies — always check mono compatibility.
source: Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio — Mike Senior (Second Edition)

role: Author — The Art of Mixing
quote: I ask my students to close their eyes and visualize the mix as a hologram. Where is each instrument in three-dimensional space? That visualization is the mix.
source: The Art of Mixing: A Visual Guide to Recording, Engineering, and Production — David Gibson

role: Author — The Art of Mixing
quote: Panning places a sound left to right. Reverb places it front to back. EQ places it up or down in the frequency spectrum. Together they define a complete three-dimensional position.
source: The Art of Mixing: A Visual Guide to Recording, Engineering, and Production — David Gibson

role: Author — The Art of Mixing
quote: Boosting high frequencies in the 10-15kHz range creates the sensation of a sound being closer to the listener. Cutting them pushes it further away. EQ is a depth tool as much as a tonal tool.
source: The Art of Mixing: A Visual Guide to Recording, Engineering, and Production — David Gibson

role: Author — The Art of Mixing
quote: The low end is mono. Low frequencies below about 80-100Hz are non-directional. Panning bass instruments does not create stereo width — it creates phase problems.
source: The Art of Mixing: A Visual Guide to Recording, Engineering, and Production — David Gibson

role: Author — The Art of Mixing
quote: Every mix tells a visual story in sound. The kick and bass form the foundation. The vocals and lead instruments are the focal point. Everything else is the landscape they inhabit.
source: The Art of Mixing: A Visual Guide to Recording, Engineering, and Production — David Gibson

role: Producer (Michael Jackson, Frank Sinatra, Ray Charles, Count Basie)
quote: I had to learn every instrument before I could produce. Not to play them all — to understand what each one could and could not do. A producer who doesn't understand the instruments is flying blind.
source: Q: The Autobiography of Quincy Jones (2001)

role: Producer (Michael Jackson, Frank Sinatra, Ray Charles, Count Basie)
quote: Frank Sinatra taught me that phrasing is everything. The note is just a container. The meaning lives in the space before and after it.
source: Q: The Autobiography of Quincy Jones (2001)

role: Producer (Michael Jackson, Frank Sinatra, Ray Charles, Count Basie)
quote: The bridge of a song is the most important moment. It's where the song turns and reveals something it was hiding. Get the bridge wrong and the whole song lies.
source: Q: The Autobiography of Quincy Jones (2001)

role: Producer (Michael Jackson, Frank Sinatra, Ray Charles, Count Basie)
quote: I score every instrument independently before I score the ensemble. Each line has to work alone before it works together. A great arrangement is not a collection of parts — it is a single organism.
source: Q: The Autobiography of Quincy Jones (2001)

role: Producer (Michael Jackson, Frank Sinatra, Ray Charles, Count Basie)
quote: Michael Jackson was the most complete artist I have ever worked with. He heard the finished record in his head before we recorded a single note. My job was to help him get it out.
source: Q: The Autobiography of Quincy Jones (2001)

role: Producer (Michael Jackson, Frank Sinatra, Ray Charles, Count Basie)
quote: I have never met a great musician who could not also listen. The ability to hear what someone else is playing and respond to it — that is the foundation of all music.
source: Q: The Autobiography of Quincy Jones (2001)

role: Producer (Michael Jackson, Frank Sinatra, Ray Charles, Count Basie)
quote: The rhythm section is the engine. The horns are the emotion. The strings are the color. The vocal is the soul. Take any one of them away and the machine still runs — but it runs incomplete.
source: Q: The Autobiography of Quincy Jones (2001)

role: Mix Engineer (Nine Inch Nails, Smashing Pumpkins, My Bloody Valentine)
quote: Flanging is the most aggressive modulation tool you have. It's the sound of metal bending. When you want something to feel like it's tearing apart at the seams, that's where you go.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 92, 2012

role: Guitarist, Producer (My Bloody Valentine)
quote: The flanger on Loveless wasn't used as an effect. It was used as a tuning system. We'd tune the flanger until the comb filtering sat inside the chord rather than fighting it.
source: Sound On Sound — My Bloody Valentine: Loveless Revisited, November 2012

role: Guitarist, Producer (Van Halen)
quote: The flanger gives you that jet plane sweep — nobody who heard Unchained on the radio in 1981 had heard a guitar sound like that before. It was the MXR 117. Simple as that.
source: Guitar World — Eddie Van Halen: The Making of Fair Warning, March 1991

role: Producer, Musician (Nine Inch Nails)
quote: Flanger is one of those effects that announces itself immediately. You can't hide it. So when we use it, we use it as a statement — not a texture.
source: Sound On Sound — Nine Inch Nails: The Fragile Sessions, October 1999

role: Guitarist, Producer (Pink Floyd)
quote: Vibrato is about breath. A note without vibrato is a held breath. With vibrato it's a voice. The depth and speed have to match the emotional weight of the phrase — not the tempo of the song.
source: Guitar Player Magazine — David Gilmour: The Voice of Pink Floyd, July 1984

role: Instrument Designer (LinnDrum, MPC)
quote: Vibrato on a synthesizer is the cheapest imitation of the human voice. Done badly it sounds mechanical. Done well — with a delayed onset and natural rate variation — it sounds alive.
source: Sound On Sound — Roger Linn: The MPC Story, June 2008

role: Musician, Producer
quote: You can't program vibrato. You have to feel where the note wants to move. On the Clavinet, the vibrato is in your wrist. On the Moog, it's in the mod wheel. Same principle, different touch.
source: Keyboard Magazine — Stevie Wonder: Master of Keys, September 1985

role: Guitarist, Producer (RCA Nashville)
quote: Tremolo is the heartbeat of country music. The Fender amp tremolo circuit was never technically perfect — the speed drifted, the depth changed with temperature — and that imperfection is exactly what made it musical.
source: Guitar Player Magazine — Chet Atkins: The Country Gentleman, April 1979

role: Musician, Producer (The White Stripes, Third Man Records)
quote: Tremolo makes a guitar nervous. It puts anxiety into a note that would otherwise just sit there. Seven Nation Army wouldn't be the same song without that tremolo — it's what makes the riff feel like a heartbeat.
source: Rolling Stone — Jack White: Third Man Standing, August 2012

role: Record Producer, Engineer (Tool, System of a Down, Prince)
quote: Tremolo is one of the most underused tools in the studio. Producers reach for reverb and delay automatically, but tremolo applied to a pad at a slow rate creates a breathing quality that no other effect can replicate.
source: Recording Unhinged — Creative and Unconventional Music Recording Techniques

role: Synthesizer Designer (Moog Music)
quote: The low-frequency oscillator is the heartbeat of a synthesizer patch. Everything else — the filter, the amplifier, the pitch — can be set in motion by a single LFO, and the relationship between the LFO rate and the note duration determines whether the result is music or noise.
source: Keyboard Magazine — Bob Moog: The Man Behind the Music, October 1994

role: Electronic Musician, Producer
quote: An LFO isn't just a modulation source. It's a second melody. When the LFO rate is close to a musical interval relative to the tempo, the modulation creates rhythmic counterpoint that isn't written anywhere — it emerges from the math.
source: The Wire Magazine — Aphex Twin: Selected Ambient Works, December 1994

role: Composer, Producer (Blade Runner, Chariots of Fire)
quote: I think of the LFO as a breathing mechanism. The synthesizer without modulation is static — it's a held note with no life. The LFO gives it lungs.
source: Sound On Sound — Vangelis: The Architecture of Sound, March 2001

role: Mastering Engineer (Digital Domain)
quote: Multiband compression is the most dangerous tool in mastering. In the wrong hands it creates a mix that measures perfectly and sounds completely artificial — each frequency range optimised in isolation, the whole destroyed in the process.
source: Mastering Audio: The Art and the Science — Bob Katz (3rd Edition, 2015)

role: Mastering Engineer, Loudness Expert
quote: Multiband compressors are incredibly seductive because they let you fix problems you couldn't fix any other way. But they also let you create problems you couldn't create any other way. The skill is knowing which is which.
source: Production Advice — Multiband Compression: The Good, The Bad, and The Ugly, 2014

role: Mix Engineer (Green Day, Muse, Bruce Springsteen)
quote: I use multiband on the mix bus to control the low-mids — that 200 to 500Hz range that gets congested in a dense arrangement. I'm not compressing the mix. I'm sculpting a specific frequency range that broadband compression can't reach without affecting everything else.
source: Sound On Sound — Chris Lord-Alge: The Mix Engineer, September 2007

role: Mix Engineer (Beyoncé, Christina Aguilera, Pink)
quote: Multiband compression on a vocal bus gives you control that no EQ can match — you're compressing the resonant frequencies only when they get loud, which is exactly when they need control. It's dynamic EQ before dynamic EQ had a name.
source: Pensado's Place — Episode 212: Vocal Processing Deep Dive, 2015

role: Mastering Engineer (Digital Domain)
quote: LUFS finally gave us a number that means something. Peak levels never told you how loud a record sounded. LUFS measures what the ear actually experiences — integrated loudness over time — and that changes everything about how we think about mastering.
source: Mastering Audio: The Art and the Science — Bob Katz (3rd Edition, 2015)

role: Mastering Engineer, Loudness Expert
quote: The loudness war is over and LUFS won. Streaming platforms normalise to a target — typically -14 LUFS integrated — which means crushing your master for perceived loudness gains you nothing on Spotify but costs you dynamic range you can never get back.
source: Production Advice — The Loudness War Is Over. Here's What It Means For You, 2016

role: Senior Sound Engineer, EBU R128 Standards Committee
quote: Loudness normalisation based on LUFS is not a creative constraint — it's the removal of a technical arms race that was damaging music. Dynamics return when the incentive to destroy them disappears.
source: AES Convention Paper — Loudness Normalisation and the End of the Loudness War, 2012

role: Record Producer (Frank Sinatra, Billy Joel, Paul Simon)
quote: The EMT 140 plate is the most musical reverb ever built. It has a density and a shimmer that no algorithm has ever matched. When you hear Sinatra on a plate, you hear a room that doesn't exist but should.
source: Sound On Sound — Phil Ramone: The Producer's Producer, May 2005

role: Recording Engineer (The Beatles, Paul McCartney)
quote: Abbey Road's plates were our primary reverb tools. Different plates had different characters — some were darker, some brighter — and we'd choose based on the source. The vocal plate and the drum plate were never the same plate.
source: Here, There and Everywhere: My Life Recording the Music of the Beatles — Geoff Emerick (2006)

role: Recording Engineer (Michael Jackson, Quincy Jones)
quote: The plate reverb on Thriller was an EMT 140. We used it on the snare primarily — that explosive crack followed by the plate tail is one of the defining sounds of that record. There is no plugin replacement. The real thing is the real thing.
source: Make Mine Music — Bruce Swedien (2003)

role: Producer, Engineer (Björk, The Killers, Austra)
quote: Convolution reverb changed everything because suddenly you could put a vocal in a real room — not a simulation of a room, an actual measured room. The Hagia Sophia, the Pantheon, a tile bathroom in 1962. The specificity is what makes it powerful.
source: Sound On Sound — Damian Taylor: Sound Placement, March 2012

role: Producer, Musician (Talking Heads, U2, David Bowie)
quote: Impulse response reverb is an act of acoustic archaeology. You're measuring a space that existed at a specific moment in time and importing it into a completely different context. That's a profound creative tool.
source: Sound On Sound — Brian Eno: Oblique Strategies, December 2004

role: Mixing Engineer, Sound On Sound Contributor
quote: The trap with convolution reverb is treating it like a hardware unit — set and forget. The best convolution results come from treating the impulse response as raw material: EQ it, gate the tail, automate the pre-delay. The IR is a starting point, not a finished sound.
source: Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio — Mike Senior (2011)

role: Recording Engineer (Pixies, Nirvana, PJ Harvey)
quote: The room is never neutral. Every room has a personality — a resonant frequency, a decay character, a relationship between the direct sound and the first reflection. Ignoring that is ignoring half your instrument.
source: Sound On Sound — Steve Albini: The Recorded Room, April 2012

role: Recording Engineer (Jimi Hendrix, Led Zeppelin, Kiss)
quote: When The Levee Breaks was recorded in the stairwell at Headley Grange with the mics two floors up. That room sound is John Bonham plus a stone stairwell — you cannot manufacture that in any reverb plugin ever built.
source: Sound On Sound — The Making of Led Zeppelin IV, December 2007

role: Record Producer (U2, Bob Dylan, Peter Gabriel)
quote: I always record in rooms with character. A room that sounds like nothing forces you to manufacture ambience artificially. A room that sounds like something gives you a starting point — and a deadline. You capture it or you miss it.
source: Soul Mining: A Musical Life — Daniel Lanois (2010)

role: Mix Engineer (Beyoncé, Christina Aguilera, Pink)
quote: Transient shapers let you sculpt the attack and sustain of a sound independently of its level — which compression can't do. You can make a snare snap harder without making the room louder. That separation of attack from body is the tool compression was always pretending to be.
source: Pensado's Place — Episode 187: Transient Processing Deep Dive, 2014

role: Mix Engineer (Pearl Jam, Tom Waits, Sheryl Crow)
quote: A transient shaper on drums changes the character of the performance without changing the performance. Reduce the attack and the drummer sounds like they're playing through a blanket. Increase it and every stroke has authority. It's a remarkable tool.
source: Tape Op Magazine Issue 63, 2008

role: Mixing Engineer, Sound On Sound Contributor
quote: Transient designers are most useful when you need to fix a problem that compression would make worse. If the attack is too aggressive, compressing adds pumping. A transient shaper reduces the attack cleanly without affecting anything else.
source: Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio — Mike Senior (2011)

role: Recording Engineer, Inventor of the Parametric EQ
quote: The parametric equaliser was designed to give engineers surgical control over the centre frequency, bandwidth, and gain of a boost or cut — independently, simultaneously. Before that, you guessed. With parametric EQ, you could be precise.
source: AES Journal — George Massenburg: Parametric Equalization, 1972

role: Mastering Engineer (Digital Domain)
quote: Parametric EQ in mastering is mostly subtractive. You're looking for the frequency that's causing a problem — a resonance, a room mode, a recording artefact — and you're removing it with the narrowest Q that gets the job done. Wide boosts are for colour. Narrow cuts are for surgery.
source: Mastering Audio: The Art and the Science — Bob Katz (3rd Edition, 2015)

role: Mix Engineer (Green Day, Muse, Bruce Springsteen)
quote: I always sweep before I cut. I boost 6dB, sweep the frequency until I find the worst-sounding point, then cut it. That's how you find the problem frequency without guessing. The parametric makes that method possible.
source: Sound On Sound — Chris Lord-Alge: Mix Approach, September 2007

role: Mixing Engineer, Sound On Sound Contributor
quote: High-pass filtering is the first thing I do on almost every channel. If a signal doesn't need energy below 80Hz — and most signals don't — removing it gives the kick and bass more room without touching their levels. It's free headroom.
source: Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio — Mike Senior (2011)

role: Mix Engineer (Red Hot Chili Peppers, Adele, Jay-Z)
quote: Every channel gets a high-pass unless there's a specific reason not to. The amount of subsonic and low-mid garbage that accumulates in a mix without high-passing is staggering — you can't hear it individually but collectively it destroys the low end.
source: Sound On Sound — Andrew Scheps: Mix Philosophy, February 2013

role: Mix Engineer (Beyoncé, Christina Aguilera, Pink)
quote: The high-pass filter is the most used tool in my mix and the least discussed. Producers spend hours on compression and EQ curves but the single most impactful thing you can do to 80% of channels is roll off below 100Hz and let the bottom breathe.
source: Pensado's Place — Episode 156: EQ Fundamentals, 2013

role: Record Producer (Frankie Goes to Hollywood, Yes, Art of Noise)
quote: A low-pass filter on a synth lead creates tension through absence. You're rolling off the top and the listener's ear leans in — it expects the brightness and doesn't get it. When you open the filter on the drop, the release is physical.
source: Sound On Sound — Trevor Horn: The Producer's Producer, July 2004

role: Electronic Music Producer
quote: The filter sweep is the oldest trick in electronic music and it works every single time because it mimics the way the ear focuses on sound. Low-pass opening up sounds like something emerging from a distance. It's primal.
source: Computer Music Magazine — Deadmau5: Production Masterclass, Issue 196, 2013

role: Electronic Music Producers
quote: We use the low-pass filter as a compositional tool — not an effect. The filter position determines the emotional state of a section. Closed is tension, open is release. It's that simple and that powerful.
source: Sound On Sound — Underworld: Everything Everything Sessions, August 2000

role: Mix Engineer (Jennifer Lopez, Marc Anthony)
quote: Clip gain is the most honest gain tool in the DAW because it happens before everything else. When I ride clip gain on a vocal, I'm editing the performance at the source — before compression, before EQ, before anything else colours the signal.
source: Puremix — Fab Dupont: Gain Staging Masterclass, 2017

role: Mixing Engineer, Sound On Sound Contributor
quote: Clip gain automation is the first stage of vocal production in my sessions. I bring every phrase to a consistent level before the compressor even sees the signal. The compressor's job then becomes tone shaping, not taming wild level swings.
source: Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio — Mike Senior (2011)

role: Record Producer, Mix Engineer (Aerosmith, The Fray)
quote: I clip-gain everything before I touch a compressor. Drums, vocals, bass — everything. A compressor working on a consistent signal behaves consistently. A compressor working on a wildly dynamic signal behaves unpredictably. Control the level first, then shape the tone.
source: Produce Like A Pro — Warren Huart: Gain Staging Workflow, 2018

role: Mix Engineer (John Mayer, Gwen Stefani, The Rolling Stones)
quote: Air is the presence of the recording space above 12kHz. When you boost that range you're not adding brightness — you're adding the room. Done right it makes everything sound like it was recorded in an amazing space. Done wrong it sounds like static.
source: Sound On Sound — Jack Joseph Puig: The Mix Engineer, January 2009

role: Mix Engineer (Green Day, Muse, Bruce Springsteen)
quote: I boost air on almost every vocal — somewhere between 14kHz and 20kHz, a gentle shelf. It adds an openness that makes the vocal feel live and present without making it harsh. The Neve 1073 does this naturally. Most plugins have to be told to do it.
source: Sound On Sound — Chris Lord-Alge: Vocal Processing, September 2007

role: Mix Engineer (Beyoncé, Christina Aguilera, Pink)
quote: The air frequency on a mix is like the sunlight hitting the room. If it's missing the mix feels closed and dark. Too much and it feels harsh and fatiguing. Finding the right amount — usually a broad shelf around 16kHz — is one of those calibrations that separates mixes that breathe from mixes that don't.
source: Pensado's Place — Episode 203: High Frequency EQ Deep Dive, 2015

role: Synthesizer Designer (Moog Music)
quote: The oscillator is the voice of the synthesizer. Its waveform is its timbre — a sine wave is pure and simple, a sawtooth is complex and buzzing, a square wave is hollow and reedy. Every other parameter in the synthesizer is shaping or modifying what the oscillator already is.
source: Keyboard Magazine — Bob Moog: Synthesis Fundamentals, March 1985

role: Musician, Keyboardist (Emerson, Lake & Palmer)
quote: The Moog oscillator drifts. Temperature, humidity, time — the tuning shifts and you have to constantly correct it. But that drift is also what makes it alive. Two oscillators slightly out of tune with each other produce a beating that no stable digital oscillator can replicate.
source: Keyboard Magazine — Keith Emerson: The Moog and Me, November 1973

role: Sound Designer, Electronic Musician
quote: When you modulate an oscillator with another oscillator you leave the territory of conventional sound design and enter frequency relationships that the ear has never heard before. That's where the interesting sounds live — in the interaction between two unstable periodic systems.
source: Computer Music Magazine — Richard Devine: Advanced Synthesis, Issue 201, 2014

role: Synthesizer Designer (Moog Music)
quote: The ADSR envelope was the key to making synthesisers expressive. Before the envelope generator, a note was either on or off. The envelope gave it a shape — a beginning, a middle, and an end — and suddenly the synthesiser could mimic the behaviour of acoustic instruments.
source: Keyboard Magazine — Bob Moog: The Envelope Generator Story, June 1988

role: Composer, Producer (Blade Runner, Chariots of Fire)
quote: The attack of an envelope is where the instrument declares itself. A fast attack is decisive — a slow attack is tentative, searching. I spend more time on the attack of a pad than on any other parameter because it determines the emotional character of the entire sound.
source: Sound On Sound — Vangelis: The Blade Runner Scores, January 2003

role: Electronic Musician, Producer
quote: Release time is the most important part of the envelope for electronic music. The release determines how the sound relates to the space around it — a long release fills the gap between notes, a short release creates silence, and silence is as important as sound.
source: The Wire Magazine — Aphex Twin: On Synthesis, March 1995

role: Record Producer (Donna Summer, Daft Punk, Blondie)
quote: The vocoder on I Feel Love was not a stylistic choice — it was the only way to get the sound we heard in our heads. The voice and the synthesiser becoming one instrument. That combination defined a decade of electronic music.
source: Sound On Sound — Giorgio Moroder: The Architect of Electronic Dance, June 2013

role: Electronic Music Producers
quote: The vocoder is the bridge between human and machine. When Thomas sings through it on Harder Better Faster Stronger, it's both of us — his voice and the synthesiser — neither one dominating the other. That's the balance we were after.
source: Rolling Stone — Daft Punk: The Robots Speak, October 2001

role: Keyboardist, Synthesist (Todd Rundgren, Utopia)
quote: The vocoder works by transferring the spectral envelope of speech onto any carrier signal. Once you understand that mechanically, you realise it doesn't have to be a voice on a synthesiser — it can be drums through strings, guitar through choir, anything through anything.
source: Keyboard Magazine — Roger Powell: Vocoder Theory and Practice, April 1980

role: Synthesizer Designer (PPG Wave)
quote: Wavetable synthesis was born from a simple question: what if you could cycle through different waveforms as a note plays? Instead of a static timbre, the sound evolves. Instead of one voice, an instrument with infinite voices in sequence.
source: Sound On Sound — Wolfgang Palm: The PPG Story, March 2014

role: Electronic Music Producers
quote: The Waldorf Microwave was our primary synthesis tool on Mezzanine. Wavetable synthesis has a particular hardness and coldness that analogue doesn't — and that coldness was exactly the emotional texture we were after.
source: Sound On Sound — Massive Attack: Mezzanine Sessions, November 1998

role: Software Developer, Sound Designer
quote: Modern wavetable synthesis is about movement through spectral space. The wavetable position isn't a static setting — it's a parameter to automate, modulate, and sequence. A sound that evolves is more interesting than a sound that holds still.
source: Ask Audio Magazine — Steve Duda: Designing Serum, 2014

role: Mathematician (Fourier Analysis)
quote: Any periodic waveform, however complex, can be expressed as a sum of sinusoidal components at harmonically related frequencies. This is the mathematical foundation of additive synthesis — and of our entire understanding of timbre.
source: Théorie analytique de la chaleur — Jean-Baptiste Joseph Fourier (1822)

role: Instrument Designer (Fairlight CMI)
quote: The Fairlight's additive synthesis engine let you draw a waveform by hand — literally paint the harmonic content with a light pen on the screen. That direct relationship between shape and sound, between vision and hearing, was unlike anything that existed before.
source: Sound On Sound — The Fairlight CMI: A History, August 2013

role: Electronic Musician, Author (Handmade Electronic Music)
quote: Additive synthesis is the most honest form of sound synthesis because it's rooted in acoustics — the same mathematics that describes a piano string or a clarinet reed. Every other synthesis method is a shortcut around it.
source: Handmade Electronic Music: The Art of Hardware Hacking — Nic Collins (2006)
